summary:
Pompey's Eastern Campaigns (66–63 BC) were a series of military and diplomatic operations in which Gnaeus Pompey resolved the Third [[event:1091|Mithridatic Wars]], reorganized Rome's eastern provinces, and extended Roman power throughout the Near East to the borders of the Parthian Empire, making Pompey the most powerful man in the Roman world and transforming Rome's eastern frontier for centuries.

Pompey was given an extraordinary command (the lex Manilia) that superseded all other Roman commanders in the east. He drove Mithridates to suicide in the Crimea and then swept through the region reorganizing territories: Pontus, Bithynia, Syria, and Judea were all brought under direct Roman control or reorganized as client kingdoms. His settlement of the [[event:1147|Hasmonean Kingdom]] of Judea — entering Jerusalem and the Temple in 63 BC, though respectfully avoiding the inner sanctum — began the entanglement between Rome and Judea that would eventually produce the Jewish Wars. He also suppressed piracy in the eastern Mediterranean and settled thousands of ex-pirates in towns, dramatically improving trade routes. His campaigns added enormous wealth to Rome's treasury and made him personally fabulously rich, giving him the resources to participate in the [[event:1073|First Triumvirate]].
